The Xbox High-Level Voice (XHV) Library

This section presents an overview of the Xbox High-Level Voice (XHV) library in the following topics.

  • Basic Concepts
  • Initialization
  • Using the XHV Library

Basic Concepts

The XHV library provides a simplified, yet powerful and flexible interface for using the voice capabilities of the Xbox on Xbox Live. The design of the library is such that anyone who is inputting voice data is called a talker. Talkers can be local or remote. Local talkers are players whose headsets are connected to the Xbox on which the title is currently running. Remote talkers are players who are connected to other Xbox consoles and whose voice data is being received via Xbox Live.

With the XHV library, your game can incorporate voice mail, live chats, and speech recognition. Used well, these capabilities enable players to easily accomplish many tasks that would normally require a keyboard. Voice input often simplifies game interfaces. It can also increase usability and player involvement.

Initialization

To begin using the XHV library, your game must first create a pointer to the XHV engine's interface. Do this by calling the XHVEngineCreate function. When your game calls XHVEngineCreate, pass it an XHV_RUNTIME_PARAMS structure to set the initialization parameters. In particular, the game must set the maximum number of local and remote talkers. By default, the maximum number of remote talkers is 30. However, it is recommended that you set a lower maximum to prevent the player's network connection from bogging down.

With the IXHVEngine pointer returned by XHVEngineCreate, your game initializes the voice engine by calling IXHVEngine::EnableProcessingMode. If your game is doing speach recognition, it next calls IXHVEngine::RegisterSpeechRecognitionBank followed by IXHVEngine::SelectVocabulary. Next, the game calls IXHVEngine::SetProcessingMode to set the processing mode for specific local talkers. Finally, it registers local talkers by calling IXHVEngine::RegisterLocalTalker and registers remote talkers using IXHVEngine::RegisterRemoteTalker.

Using the XHV Library

The most important task that must be performed for proper functioning of the XHV library is to call the IXHVEngine::DoWork method. This method "pumps" the voice processing task. This should be done a minimum of 30 times per sectiond. Therefore, your game must call IXHVEngine::DoWork in its main processing loop.

To find out which remote users are in a conversation with a local player, call IXHVEngine::GetRemoteTalkers. Your game can determine whether a remote player is talking by calling IXHVEngine::IsTalking. If a remote player is talking, your game invokes IXHVEngine::SubmitIncomingVoicePacket to send the incoming voice data for that remote player to the engine. This call is made in the game's main loop after calling IXHVEngine::DoWork.

Voice mail functionality is implemented with the IXHVEngine::VoiceMailPlay, IXHVEngine::VoiceMailRecord, and IXHVEngine::VoiceMailStop.

Unpublished work. © 2000 Microsoft Corporation. All rights reserved.
